caption:
Until 1993, her medicine was only tested on him.

Today, women are still:
- At higher risk of delayed or missed diagnoses than men.
- Waiting longer for pain medication. 
- 50% more likely to be misdiagnosed during a heart attack. 

And the list goes on.
But she isn’t just a number.

She’s your mom, your partner, your sister.
She innovates. She creates. She heals.
She builds companies and communities.
And whether she’s changing the entire world, or just yours, her health matters.

And the downstream effect is still being felt. Most hormone reference ranges used in labs today were established from predominantly male study populations. So when a woman's ferritin, testosterone, or thyroid markers are evaluated against those ranges, the "normal" flag may mean nothing about what is actually optimal for her biology. 1993 was a start, but the lab ranges haven't fully caught up.

Two problems with FunctionHealth. Their analysis and customer support is heavily AI based. They also renew your account without telling you the money is coming out. Good luck trying to speak to an actual person.
And why did you exclude progesterone from your annual test? It is only available as an add-on. For menopausal women that is important to check.

**yt women. Black and brown women are still excluded from much of current testing, and there are significant disparities between treatment and health outcomes that need to be addressed. We still have so far to go.

caption:
HbA1c is your 3-month blood sugar average.

Glucose sticks to your red blood cells and stays there. The higher your blood sugar, the more sticks.

What can help lower it? Consider:
 • Eating in an 8-hour window
 • Starting with fiber and protein
 • Taking short walks after meals

Consistency matters. 

Test HbA1c with Function and see patterns, not snapshots.
firstComment:
Living with diabetes for 10 years now and wow does it make a difference in how you eat and what you do afterwards. More fibre and veg with meals, and eat the veg first, then your protein and starch/carbs last to slow absorbing in blood stream and prevent spikes. 
Go for a walk after meals. Even just 15 minutes. I walk my dog and it helps me get gas out and prevents me from getting sleepy after a meal. 
Recently I heard simply flexing your muscles like little calf raises while under your desk can help your body burn sugars. But I love my walks, rain or shine.

A1c can be false low. Short life of red blood cell. Or due to the reactive hypoglycemia - glucose dipping too low and giving lower numbers as 3 months average. CGM and fasting insulin are the best way to actually understand your glucose metabolism.

caption:
Science says a long life isn’t just built in the kitchen or the gym.
It's built in the dark. In your bed.

Sleeping less than 7 hours is linked to shorter life expectancy.

In a nationwide study, insufficient sleep was one of the strongest predictors of life expectancy—second only to smoking.

The problem? It can be the first thing sacrificed when life gets busy.

And sleep influences core systems: 
- Inflammation: hs-CRP 
- Metabolic health: Glucose, HbA1c, Insulin 
- Immune health: White blood cells
- Stress: Cortisol 

Sleep isn’t optional.
Treat it like your health depends on it—because it does.

Citations in bio. 

Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional with any medical questions. For marketing purposes only.

caption:
Brain fog is a red flag for deeper imbalances.

Your brain health is interconnected to the health of your body. When one system falls out of sync, your brain may feel the effects.

When brain fog strikes, it can mean:
- Confusion
- Fatigue
- Forgetfulness
- Losing your train of thought
- Slow thought process & reaction time
- Difficulty concentrating
- Lack of mental clarity

What’s causing it? Potential triggers include:
- Nutritional deficiencies
- Thyroid dysfunction
- Autoimmunity
- Inflammation
- Metabolic issues
- Stress
- Hormonal changes
- Long-COVID

Testing key biomarkers through Function can help you get to the root.

caption:
Had a great conversation with my personal doctor and good buddy, @drmarkhyman. 

A lot of guys wait until something really goes wrong before they check in on their health. 

I didn’t want to be one of them.

I’ve always tried to be a disciplined guy, and most days - I feel great. But something was off—and I couldn’t quite crack it.

I was introduced to Dr. Hyman through a mutual friend and discovered there’s a whole lot of value in being proactive, asking questions, digging deeper to uncover root causes and taking control of your own health. 

I’ve always prioritized my fitness, my nutrition and my mental strength - those core things have been the anchor that allows me to do everything else I love to do - be creative, be a dad, have fun. But doing some of this comprehensive testing challenged what I thought ‘health’ looked like for me and it’s been a really encouraging and eye-opening experience. 

If any of this resonates with you - this conversation between myself and Dr. Hyman is for you. 

Enjoy and let me know your thoughts.

caption:
What if the best time to find cancer isn’t when you have symptoms — but decades before?

A quiet revolution is happening in medical imaging. AI is now capable of reducing false positive rates by an order of magnitude simply by reading your images in context over time. That changes everything about how we think about prevention.

Dr. Daniel Sodickson — physicist, radiologist, and Chief Medical Scientist at @function — breaks down how MRI scans combined with blood biomarkers can predict serious disease years in advance.

This episode is for anyone who wants to stop reacting to disease and start understanding where their body is headed before it’s too late.

Find the full conversation on The Dr. Hyman Show Podcast 4/22/26 wherever you listen 🎧

caption:
What if the food you’ve eaten your whole life has been quietly driving inflammation that’s holding you back?

NBA legend @cp3 joins me to share how food sensitivities, gut dysfunction, and chronic inflammation nearly derailed a 21-year career — and what he discovered when he finally got his health data.

From eliminating 27 red-flag foods to going plant-based to watching his father’s biomarkers transform with a few simple changes, Chris breaks down the exact shifts that kept him competing at 40 against players half his age.

This one is for athletes, for anyone with a family history of chronic disease, and for every person who’s ever assumed their symptoms are just part of getting older.

caption:
3 heart health labs not on the standard panel everyone should know.

1. Apolipoprotein B (ApoB) - ApoB tells you the total number of atherogenic particles in your bloodstream. Every LDL, VLDL, IDL, and Lp(a) particle carries one ApoB molecule.

2. Lipoprotein (a) - is a genetically inherited risk factor for cardiovascular disease. Roughly 1 in 5 people have elevated Lp(a). Most people never get tested.

3. C Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) - is an indication of your general inflammation levels. Inflammation is a known driver for many diseases.

@function gives you access to 160+ lab tests to get deeper insights into your health. Join today for $365/year ($1/day) and take control. Link in bio.

caption:
Most people know how to operate their iPhone better than they know how to operate their own body.

That is about to change.

I sat down with @fidjissimo, the CEO of Applications at OpenAI, to talk about the launch of ChatGPT Health, and why this could be a turning point for how we prevent disease, navigate chronic illness, and actually understand what our symptoms are trying to tell us.

Fidji has lived the reality of “mystery symptoms” firsthand, POTS, chronic fatigue, the endless specialist carousel, and the frustration of being treated like a collection of parts instead of one connected system.

Now she is helping build the tool millions are already turning to for health answers, and doing it with one goal, empowerment.

Episode is live on The Dr. Hyman Show 2/4/26 - stay tuned! 🎧

caption:
What if the right medical decision depended on seeing your full health history in real time?

@fidjissimo, CEO of Applications at OpenAI, describes how a standard antibiotic could have reactivated a serious past infection. By quickly checking the medication against her complete health records, AI flagged the risk and pointed to a safer alternative, something no one in the room had time to catch.

That moment reveals a deeper problem in modern medicine. Care is fragmented, time is limited, and critical context often gets lost, even when the stakes are high.

When we stop treating the body as disconnected parts and start seeing it as one system, better decisions become possible.
